step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("can HIV point-of-care tests be used on sputum for detection?")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

After reviewing the retrieved evidence, I conclude:
\boxed{B}
Rationale: There is no scientific basis or validation for conducting HIV point-of-care tests on sputum. All referenced materials emphasize that HIV testing relies on biological fluids such as blood or saliva, where viral antigens or antibodies are present in sufficient concentrations. Sputum contains minimal or undetectable amounts of HIV particles and lacks any established protocol for HIV detection via standard or point-of-care assays. Although molecular tests like NAATs can detect TB directly from sputum, these do not apply to HIV. Furthermore, even though some studies mention HIV-associated symptoms (e.g., leukopenia) found in sputum analyses indirectly through immune responses, this does not validate the use of sputum for actual HIV detection. Therefore, attempting to perform an HIV POCT on sputum would yield inaccurate and unreliable results, rendering it ineffective for screening purposes.\boxed{B} After reviewing the retrieved evidence, I conclude:
\boxed{B}

Sputum contains minimal or undetectable amounts of HIV particles and lacks any established protocol for HIV detection via standard or point-of-care assays. Although molecular tests like NAATs can detect TB directly from sputum, these do not apply to HIV. Furthermore, even though some studies mention HIV-associated symptoms (e.g., leukopenia) found in sputum analyses indirectly through immune responses, this does not validate the use of sputum for actual HIV detection. Therefore, attempting to perform an HIV POCT on sputum would yield inaccurate and unreliable results, rendering it ineffective for screening purposes. Based on the above rationale, what is the correct option?
